what_it_does: Defines the design-token layer for UI states: loading skeletons with shimmer, designed empty/error zero-states, and a consistent interaction-state (hover/press/selected/disabled) + keyboard focus-ring language. Provides CSS custom properties under :root plus utility classes (.skeleton, .state-block, .interactive) and a :focus-visible rule.
observations: Skeleton tokens (--skeleton-base/-sheen/-radius) and a cvShimmer keyframe that slides a sheen gradient; .skeleton sets color:transparent !important to hide placeholder text
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) disables the shimmer and drops to static opacity 0.5
Focus tokens use --accent-gold for the ring; a :where(...) selector applies the ring only on :focus-visible (keyboard), explicitly not on mouse press
.state-block is a designed zero-state with a circular glyph slot, title, body, dashed border, max-width 42ch, tintable by a .zone-* class via --zone-ink

.interactive centralises hover (--state-hover), active/press (--state-press with a 0.5px translateY nudge), disabled (opacity + pointer-events:none), and selected (aria-selected/data-selected → --state-selected)
State colors built with color-mix(in oklch, var(--ink) N%, transparent) so they ride the ink ramp rather than fixed greys
standouts: Stated design principle 'nothing teleports — content RESOLVES from a placeholder, never pops in' tying skeletons to the motion language
Skeleton-to-content handoff via an .enter-* class (referenced, defined elsewhere) so loading morphs into content
color-mix in oklch against --ink means states auto-recompute per theme without re-declaration
conventions: Follows token-then-utility layering and var() referencing with fallbacks (var(--r-sm, 6px))
Uses oklch color-mix and aria-attribute-driven state selectors (aria-disabled, aria-selected)
Carries a /* @kind other */ annotation on --disabled-opacity, matching the corpus token-kind tagging
concerns: color:transparent !important on .skeleton could mask content if the .enter-* swap or class removal fails
Several referenced tokens (--ink, --accent-gold, --vi-strong, --dur-quick, --ease-out, --space-fluid-lg, --fs-h4) are assumed defined elsewhere; this file does not self-contain them
notes: The states/feedback token file: skeletons that shimmer-then-resolve, designed empty/error blocks, and one .interactive class carrying hover/press/select/disabled + a keyboard-only gold focus ring.
questions: Where is the .enter-* class family that swaps skeleton placeholders into resolved content defined?
What sets --zone-ink for the .state-block glyph tint, and how do .zone-* wrappers map to error vs success?
purpose_vs_actual: Match — the header comment promises skeletons, empty/error states, and an interaction+focus language, and the file delivers exactly those tokens and classes.
apparent_themes: Designed zero-states instead of bare grey text
Single consistent interaction + accessibility (focus-visible, reduced-motion) language
Theme-aware color via oklch mixing toward the ink ramp
intention_signals: Trying to make every loading/empty/error/interaction moment feel authored and consistent across the whole system from one token home, so feedback states inherit the design DNA rather than being hand-set per component.
novelty: The skeleton-as-motion-partner framing ('nothing teleports') and the keyboard-only gold focus ring expressed purely through :focus-visible + oklch ink-mixing.
contribution: Contributes the system's state/feedback vocabulary — shimmer skeletons, designed empty/error blocks, and a reusable .interactive hover/press/select/disabled + accessible focus-ring layer keyed to the ink ramp and reduced-motion.
summary_for_embedding: A ConceptV design-system CSS token file defining loading skeletons with shimmer, designed empty/error zero-states, and a unified interaction-state and keyboard focus-ring language built on oklch ink-ramp color-mixing.
intention_for_embedding: To give the design system one authored home for loading, empty, error, and interaction feedback so content resolves rather than pops and every state inherits the design language consistently.
